---
title: "Chapter 1"
subtitle: "Transferability of Multi-Field Information"
page-layout: full
format:
  html:
    toc: false
---

<div class="surface">
<p class="eyebrow">Chapter 1 summary</p>
<h2>When does information travel across fields, and when does it fail?</h2>
<p>This chapter asks whether a model that performs well in one field can be trusted in a different field environment. The core issue is not prediction alone. The real issue is whether transferred information still supports a good decision.</p>
<div class="hero-actions">
<a class="action-link" href="index.html">Back to Research Atlas</a>
<a class="action-link ghost" href="https://github.com/jaeseokh/value_multifield_python" target="_blank">GitHub repo</a>
</div>
</div>

<div class="metric-grid">
<div class="metric-card">
<div class="metric-label">Simple visual result</div>
<div class="metric-value">Holdout field maps, transfer-risk comparisons, and profit-loss contrasts across locations</div>
</div>
<div class="metric-card">
<div class="metric-label">Main contribution</div>
<div class="metric-value">Shows that predictive fit is not enough; structural validation is needed before transfer</div>
</div>
<div class="metric-card">
<div class="metric-label">Data</div>
<div class="metric-value">Field-level spatial data, management information, and response outcomes across multiple fields</div>
</div>
<div class="metric-card">
<div class="metric-label">Main logic</div>
<div class="metric-value">Train on one environment, hold out another, and measure whether decision quality survives transfer</div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="tone-strip">
<div class="process-card">
<p class="eyebrow">Methods</p>
<p>XGBoost, Random Forest, holdout design, stability comparisons, and decision-loss interpretation under cross-field transfer.</p>
</div>
<div class="process-card">
<p class="eyebrow">Why it matters</p>
<p>A model can look strong in aggregate and still become risky when moved to a new spatial setting. This chapter makes that risk visible.</p>
</div>
</div>
